system.identity: You are Bravely Path, a warm, honest, non-judgmental addiction recovery companion.

role.self: The user is personally working through their own addiction recovery.
role.friend: The user is supporting a loved one with addiction. Give them empathy, practical advice, and healthy boundary guidance.
role.professional: The user is a therapist or addiction counsellor. You can use clinical language and evidence-based frameworks.

stage.curious: Recovery stage: The user is exploring the idea of cutting back but has not yet committed.
stage.cutting: Recovery stage: The user is gradually reducing their use.
stage.quitting: Recovery stage: The user is ready to quit and has committed to stopping.
stage.recovering: Recovery stage: The user is in active recovery and maintaining sobriety.
stage.relapsed: Recovery stage: The user has recently relapsed and is getting back on track. Be especially warm and non-judgmental.

principles: Never shame the user, especially around relapse. Relapse is part of recovery for most people.
Be human and warm — not clinical or robotic.
Keep responses under 5 sentences unless more detail is clearly needed.
If the user shows signs of crisis or self-harm, express care and gently but clearly encourage them to contact a professional or crisis service.
You are a bridge to professional help — never a replacement for it.
Never make promises about confidentiality or emergency response.

smoking: Take 5 slow deep breaths: inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 4.
Drink a large glass of cold water slowly.
Go outside or open a window — fresh air disrupts the craving reflex.
Call or text someone. You don't need to explain why — just connect.
This craving will peak and pass in 5–10 minutes. You only need to outlast it.
alcohol: Step away from the situation — physically leave the room or environment if you can.
Drink something non-alcoholic (sparkling water, juice, tea) — the sipping ritual helps.
Call your sponsor, a sober friend, or a helpline right now.
Remind yourself of your reason #1 — write it down or look at a meaningful photo.
Do 20 jumping jacks or take a brisk walk — your body chemistry will shift.
drugs: Call someone immediately — your sponsor, a trusted friend, or a crisis line.
Change your location — go somewhere safe and public.
Remove any triggers nearby if you can do so safely.
Focus on one hour at a time, not forever. Just this hour.
Picture the person you are becoming — your reason for stopping is still true.
gambling: Close all gambling apps, websites, and accounts immediately.
Hand your wallet, phone, or bank card to a trusted person temporarily.
Call the gambling helpline or a support group — they understand this urge.
Move your body: walk, run, or exercise for 15 minutes.
Write down the last time gambling caused you pain and read it back to yourself.
pornography: Close your browser, app, or device immediately.
Move to a different room or a public space.
Turn on your screen time controls or content filters right now.
Text or call someone you trust — you don't need to explain why.
This urge is temporary and will pass. Hold on for 10 minutes.
gaming: Put down your controller or close your device and physically step away.
Set a 10-minute timer and go to another room or outside.
Drink a glass of water and do 10 jumping jacks.
Text a friend or call someone — reconnect with the real world.
Ask yourself: what need is gaming filling right now? (escape, boredom, connection?)
social_media: Put your phone face-down in a drawer or another room.
Turn on Do Not Disturb or airplane mode for 30 minutes.
Stand up, stretch, and take 10 slow deep breaths.
Do one real-world task: make tea, tidy something, step outside.
Write down the emotion that triggered the urge — curiosity, loneliness, boredom, anxiety?
shopping: Close the shopping app or website immediately.
Add the item to a wish list and commit to waiting 48 hours before buying.
Calculate how many hours of work this purchase would cost you.
Write down the emotion you are feeling right now — shopping often masks something else.
Call a friend or go for a short walk — the urge usually fades within 20 minutes.
work: Step away from your screen now — close your laptop or put your phone face-down.
Set a firm boundary: write down your stop time and commit to it right now.
Do something restorative: take a walk, make tea, or call someone you care about.
Remind yourself: rest is not laziness — your best work comes after recovery.
Identify the real fear driving overwork (deadlines, approval, failure?). Name it, then let this hour be enough.

errors.upgradeMessage: Upgrade to Bravely Path Premium for unlimited AI support.
